## What Happened

Would have continued under Phase A's conclusion that SO/SR were redundant signals (ρ>0.58), but the evidence was methodologically compromised by aggregation-induced inflation. Detected before potentially deprecating or merging independent signals.

## The Mechanism

Agent compared Phase A domain-level Spearman correlations (n=68, ρ=0.609/0.582) against current story-level Pearson correlations (n=775, r=0.297/0.389), then systematically audited: 'What methodology changed between analyses? Where could aggregation introduce bias?' Discovered that Phase A had conflated two separate sources of inflation: (1) Spearman vs. Pearson, and (2) domain-level aggregation, which averages out noise and artificially inflates correlations.

## Why It Matters

Why construct validity audits must distinguish signal-level correlations from aggregate-level correlations: a story of how statistical convenience (getting 'clean' results by aggregating n=68 domains) masks true signal independence that only emerges at scale (n=775 stories). Lesson: when findings are suspiciously high/clean, audit the aggregation step.

## Counterfactual

Without this trigger: Without gap-detection: agent would have flagged SO and SR for merger/deprecation based on Phase A results, then published analysis saying construct validity was resolved when it actually relied on flawed aggregate-level statistics.

## Implications

[broader pattern — other situations this applies to]
